title: Canadian GP Winners and Losers: Aygen's Perfect Storm, Sam Fact's Redemption, and Alpine's Heartbreak
meta: Published: 18 May 2026 | By Lena Müller, Strategy Analyst | F1CC News
caption: Eren Aygen won his third career race in Montreal — all from P6 on the grid — as the rain shuffled the strategic deck.
body:
Let's rewind to lap 11, the pit lane entry. The rain has just started falling, light droplets catching the sun over the Circuit Gilles Villeneuve. Richard Hicks dives into the pits for intermediate tyres. He is the first to do so. Moments later, Sam Fact and Lukas Wright follow. The drivers who wait — Jason Roy, Eetu Väisänen, Eren Aygen — stay out on softs, gambling that the shower will pass. It doesn't. By the time the overcutters pit, the early stoppers have gained ten positions. Hicks leads. Sam Fact is third. The race has been turned on its head, and from that moment, the Canadian Grand Prix belongs to those who read the weather correctly — and punishes those who didn't.

Winners

Eren Aygen (Williams, P1 from P6): Aygen didn't pit first, but he pitted smartly. When the rain intensified, he was among the leaders in switching to intermediates, emerging in third. From there, he stalked Hicks, took the lead on lap 25 with a brave move around the outside of Turn 1, and never looked back. His final stint on medium tyres was a masterclass in control — building an 11‑second lead and managing it to the flag. He now has three career wins, all from P6 on the grid. "When I take pole, it's like the worst race of the time," he joked afterwards. The stat defies logic, but the result does not: Williams are back on top of the constructors' championship, and Aygen is just two points off the drivers' lead.

Robin Keisner (Audi, P2 from P13): Keisner's race was a study in resilience. He started 13th, lost ground in the early pit-stop shuffle, but refused to fade. His move to hard tyres for the final stint was perfectly judged — they came alive just as the mediums around him began to wilt. His pass on Sam Fact for second place on lap 67, around the outside of Turn 8, was the overtake of the race. With Eetu Väisänen finishing fifth, Keisner now leads the drivers' championship — a remarkable achievement for a driver who was 19th in 2024. "And that's P2," his engineer told him. Keisner's reply was pure joy: "Yess guys this feels like redemption, I want to give Aygen a high five on the podium."

Sam Fact (Aston Martin, P3 from P8): Sam Fact had not scored a point all season. He had not stood on a podium since Bahrain 2025. His team put him on hard tyres during the rain interlude — a gamble that could have backfired spectacularly. Instead, it delivered a tactical masterclass. As others struggled with graining mediums, Fact's hards stayed consistent, and he held off a charging Keisner for lap after lap before finally succumbing. Third place is his first podium with Aston Martin, and the scenes in the garage — mechanics in tears, engineers embracing — told the story of a team that has endured a brutal start to the year and finally had something to celebrate.

W. Vandesteene (Mercedes, P4 from P19): Vandesteene started last after a disastrous qualifying. He finished fourth, gaining 15 positions. Mercedes' upgraded suspension finally showed promise, and Vandesteene's patience through the rain phase — staying out just long enough, then pitting at the perfect moment — was a reminder of his racecraft. In a season of frustration for the Silver Arrows, this was a welcome bright spot.

The early intermediate stoppers (Hicks, Fact, Wright): The decision to pit on lap 11 for inters, before the rain intensified and the pit lane became chaos, was the single most important strategic call of the race. Hicks led laps and briefly looked capable of winning before a sticking wheel at his second stop dropped him to tenth. But the principle was sound: the early stoppers gained track position, and even if they couldn't all hold it, they put themselves in contention when others were left buried in traffic.

Losers

Alpine (Kondra DNF, Maradöner P8): Julian Kondra's weekend began with a dream: pole position, his first in Formula 1. It ended with his car in the wall at Turn 5, the rear suspension shattered, his helmet in his hands as he walked back to the paddock. Maradöner, meanwhile, reported mechanical issues throughout the race and could only salvage eighth. From a front-row lockout in qualifying to a double disaster on Sunday, this was a brutal reality check for a team that has shown flashes of brilliance but lacks the consistency to sustain a championship challenge.

Ferrari (Väisänen P5, Swansson P13): Väisänen started fourth and had the pace to contend — his late-race charge on soft tyres was the fastest on track — but the strategy let him down. The decision to stay out on dry tyres during the rain shower cost him positions he could not fully recover. He leaves Montreal still in the championship fight, but his lead is gone. Ferrari brought no upgrades for the second consecutive weekend, and the decision is starting to look costly. Swansson, meanwhile, was anonymous in 13th.

Richard Hicks (Audi, P10 from P2): Hicks was the early leader. He was the first to pit for inters, the first to lead on merit. But a disastrous second stop — a mechanic slamming the tyre to the ground in frustration — cost him a likely podium. Tenth place is a poor reward for a weekend of such promise. He remains a rookie, and these moments will come, but the sting will linger.

Maikel Zwart (Mercedes, P16 from pit lane): "Guys, problems again, it's getting worse now." Zwart's radio message captured the essence of his season: mechanical issues, strategic confusion, and the slow erosion of hope. He started from the pit lane, fought briefly into the midfield, and then faded as the car's gremlins returned. Mercedes are still searching for answers. Zwart is still waiting for a clean weekend.

Jason Roy (Red Bull, P12 from P5): Roy was fast in the rain, charging through the field on intermediate tyres and briefly looking like a podium threat. But a spin under braking — a lock‑up into Turn 1 — dropped him out of contention. He recovered to 12th, but the momentum from his Miami victory has dissipated. Red Bull remain inconsistent, and Roy's championship hopes are fading with every missed opportunity.

Montreal delivered a race of strategic gambles and emotional extremes. Williams are leading the constructors' championship again. Keisner has the drivers' lead. Sam Fact has reminded the paddock that he still belongs. And the circus heads to Monaco with the title fight more open than ever. The walls of the principality are waiting.
